web.title: livegrep/livegrep: Interactively grep source code. Source for ... - GitHub
web.description: Livegrep is a tool, partially inspired by Google Code Search, for interactive regex search of ~gigabyte-scale source repositories.

monthFeb 10, 2026 | | [src](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/tree/main/src "src") | [src](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/tree/main/src "src") | [Fix indexing of git repositories with submodules (](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/commit/44b2fb62ac4685ab3070f030d7130a21c2f67e31 "Fix indexing of git repositories with submodules (#410) This commit fixes a regression caused by commit 1e47ee8a11e3dad8554faa70785dedc15b80b195. In case of a git repository with submodules, the git_tree returns git_tree_entry objects of type GIT_OBJECT_COMMIT that represent the submodules, but cannot be converted to a git_object by git_tree_entry_to_object(), because the commit is part of the submodule and not of the repo itself. This commit just moves the check, if the git_tree_entry_to_object() call was successful, into the branches where the returned git_object is actually used. For the GIT_OBJECT_COMMIT entries only the path is used, not the git_object, therefor no check is needed there.") [#410](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/pull/410) [)](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/commit/44b2fb62ac4685ab3070f030d7130a21c2f67e31 "Fix indexing of git repositories with submodules (#410) This commit fixes a regression caused by commit 1e47ee8a11e3dad8554faa70785dedc15b80b195. In case of a git repository with submodules, the git_tree returns git_tree_entry objects of type GIT_OBJECT_COMMIT that represent the submodules, but cannot be converted to a git_object by git_tree_entry_to_object(), because the commit is part of the submodule and not of the repo itself. This commit just moves the check, if the git_tree_entry_to_object() call was successful, into the branches where the returned git_object is actually used. For the GIT_OBJECT_COMMIT entries only the path is used, not the git_object, therefor no check is needed there.") | 2 months agoJan 6, 2026 | | [test](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/tree/main/test "test") | [test](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/tree/main/test "test") | [Update to Ubuntu 24.04 and newer everything (](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/commit/3135ca259ffc23a84df406a72ad7758373b21693 "Update to Ubuntu 24.04 and newer everything (#404)") [#404](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/pull/404) [)](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/commit/3135ca259ffc23a84df406a72ad7758373b21693 "Update to Ubuntu 24.04 and newer everything (#404)") | 8 months agoJul 20, 2025 | | [third\_party](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/tree/main/third_party "third_party") | [third\_party](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/tree/main/third_party "third_party") | [Update to Ubuntu 24.04 and newer everything (](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/commit/3135ca259ffc23a84df406a72ad7758373b21693 "Update to Ubuntu 24.04 and newer everything (#404)") [#404](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/pull/404) [)](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/commit/3135ca259ffc23a84df406a72ad7758373b21693 "Update to Ubuntu 24.04 and newer everything (#404)") | 8 months agoJul 20, 2025 | | [tools](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/tree/main/tools "tools") | [tools](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/tree/main/tools "tools") | [Support queries with multiple path: terms (](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/commit/ffafce81807a1505a69da23eb3b0c090fcebd1bc "Support queries with multiple path: terms (#380) * Upgrade abseil for Clang 15 (macOS Sonoma) compatibility macOS Sonoma brought along a new Xcode version, which broke some things. [travisdeps.sh](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/blob/main/travisdeps.sh "travisdeps.sh") | [Add bazel build in Travis](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/commit/23f562698031f74a658553050c20efcc4870fe99 "Add bazel build in Travis") | 10 years agoAug 18, 2016 | | View all files | ## Repository files navigation # Livegrep [![Build Status](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/actions/workflows/ci.yaml/badge.svg?branch=main)](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/actions/workflows/ci.yaml) [Permalink: Livegrep ](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep#livegrep-) Livegrep is a tool, partially inspired by Google Code Search, for interactive regex search of ~gigabyte-scale source repositories. You can see a running instance at [http://livegrep.com/](http://livegrep.com/). ## Building [Permalink: Building](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep#building) livegrep builds using [bazel](http://www.bazel.io/). You will need to [install](http://www.bazel.io/docs/install.html) with a version matching that in `.bazelversion`. Running bazel via [bazelisk](https://bazel.build/install/bazelisk) will download the right version automatically. livegrep vendors and/or fetches all of its dependencies using `bazel`, and so should only require a relatively recent C++ compiler to build. Once you have those dependencies, you can build using ``` bazel build //... ``` Note that the initial build will download around 100M of dependencies. These will be cached once downloaded. ## Invoking [Permalink: Invoking](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep#invoking) To run `livegrep`, you need to invoke both the `codesearch` backend index/search process, and the `livegrep` web interface. To run the sample web interface over livegrep itself, once you have built both `codesearch` and `livegrep`: In one terminal, start the `codesearch` server like so: ``` bazel-bin/src/tools/codesearch -grpc localhost:9999 doc/examples/livegrep/index.json ``` In another, run livegrep: ``` bazel-bin/cmd/livegrep/livegrep_/livegrep ``` In a browser, now visit [http://localhost:8910/](http://localhost:8910/), and you should see a working livegrep. ## Using Index Files [Permalink: Using Index Files](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep#using-index-files) The `codesearch` binary is responsible for reading source code, maintaining an index, and handling searches. `livegrep` is stateless and relies only on the connection to `codesearch` over a TCP connection. By default, `codesearch` will build an in-memory index over the repositories specified in its configuration file. You can, however, also instruct it to save the index to a file on disk. This has the dual advantages of allowing indexes that are too large to fit in RAM, and of allowing an index file to be reused. You instruct `codesearch` to generate an index file via the `-dump_index` flag and to not launch a search server via the `-index_only` flag: ``` bazel-bin/src/tools/codesearch -index_only -dump_index livegrep.idx doc/examples/livegrep/index.json ``` Once `codeseach` has built the index, this index file can be used for future runs. Index files are standalone, and you no longer need access to the source code repositories, or even a configuration file, once an index has been built. You can just launch a search server like so: ``` bazel-bin/src/tools/codesearch -load_index livegrep.idx -grpc localhost:9999 ``` The schema for the `codesearch` configuration file defined using protobuf in [src/proto/config.proto](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/blob/main/src/proto/config.proto). ## `livegrep` [Permalink: livegrep](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep#livegrep) The `livegrep` frontend accepts an optional position argument indicating a JSON configuration file; See [doc/examples/livegrep/server.json](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/blob/main/doc/examples/livegrep/server.json) for an example, and [server/config/config.go](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/blob/main/server/config/config.go) for documentation of available options. By default, `livegrep` will connect to a single local codesearch instance on port `9999`, and listen for HTTP connections on port `8910`. ## github integration [Permalink: github integration](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep#github-integration) `livegrep` includes a helper driver, `livegrep-github-reindex`, which can automatically update and index selected github repositories. To download and index all of my repositories (except for forks), storing the repos in `repos/` and writing `nelhage.idx`, you might run: ``` bazel-bin/cmd/livegrep-github-reindex/livegrep-github-reindex -user=nelhage -forks=false -name=github.com/nelhage -out nelhage.idx ``` You can now use `nelhage.idx` as an argument to `codesearch -load_index`. ## Local repository browser [Permalink: Local repository browser](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep#local-repository-browser) `livegrep` provides the ability to view source files directly in `livegrep`, as an alternative to linking files to external viewers. This was initially implemented by @jboning [here](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/pull/70). There are a few ways to enable this. The most important steps are to 1. Generate a config file that `livegrep` can use to figure out where your source files are (locally). 2. Pass this config file as an argument to the frontend (`-index-config`) ### Generating index manually [Permalink: Generating index manually](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep#generating-index-manually) See [doc/examples/livegrep/server.json](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/blob/main/doc/examples/livegrep/server.json) for an example config file, and [server/config/config.go](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/blob/main/server/config/config.go) for documentation on available options. To enable the file viewer, you must include an [`IndexConfig`](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/blob/main/server/config/config.go#L61) block inside of the config file. An example `IndexConfig` block can be seen at [doc/examples/livegrep/index.json](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/blob/main/doc/examples/livegrep/index.json). _Tip: For each repository included in your `IndexConfig`, make sure to include `metadata.url_pattern` if you would like the file viewer to be able to link out to the external host. You'll see a warning in your browser console if you don't do this._ ### Generating index with `livegrep-github-reindex` [Permalink: Generating index with livegrep-github-reindex](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep#generating-index-with-livegrep-github-reindex) If you are already using the `livegrep-github-reindex` tool, an IndexConfig index file is generated for you, by default named "livegrep.json". Run the indexer ```` bazel-bin/cmd/livegrep-github-reindex/livegrep-github-reindex_/livegrep-github-reindex -user=xvandish -forks=false -name=github.com/xvandish -out xvandish.idx ``` ```` The indexer will have done these main things: 1. Clone (or update) all repositories for `user=xvandish` to/in `repos/xvandish` 2. Create an IndexConfig file - `repos/livegrep.json` 3. Create a code index, this is whats used to search - `./xvandish.idx` Here's an abbreviated version of what your directory might look like after running the indexer. ``` livegrep │ xvandish.idx └───repos │ │ livegrep.json │ └───xvandish │ └───repo1 │ └───repo2 │ └───repo3 ``` ### Using your generated index [Permalink: Using your generated index](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep#using-your-generated-index) Now that you generated an index file, it's time to run livegrep with it. Run the backend: ``` bazel-bin/src/tools/codesearch -load_index xvandish.idx -grpc localhost:9999 ``` Run the frontend in another shell instance with the path to the index file located at `repos/livegrep.json`. ``` bazel-bin/cmd/livegrep/livegrep_/livegrep -index-config ./repos/livegrep.json ``` In a browser, now visit `http://localhost:8910` and you should see a working livegrep. Search for something, and once you get a result, click on the file name or a line number. You should now be taken to the file browser! ## Docker images [Permalink: Docker images](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep#docker-images) Livegrep's CI builds Docker images [into the livegrep\\ organization](https://github.com/orgs/livegrep/packages) docker repository on every merge to `main`. They should be generally usable. For instance, to build+run a livegrep index of this repository, you could run: ``` docker run -v $(pwd):/data ghcr.io/livegrep/livegrep/indexer /livegrep/bin/livegrep-github-reindex -repo livegrep/livegrep -http -dir /data docker network create livegrep docker run -d --rm -v $(pwd):/data --network livegrep --name livegrep-backend ghcr.io/livegrep/livegrep/base /livegrep/bin/codesearch -load_index /data/livegrep.idx -grpc 0.0.0.0:9999 docker run -d --rm --network livegrep --publish 8910:8910 ghcr.io/livegrep/livegrep/base /livegrep/bin/livegrep -docroot /livegrep/web -listen 0.0.0.0:8910 --connect livegrep-backend:9999 ``` And then access [http://localhost:8910/](http://localhost:8910/) You can also find the [docker-compose config powering\\ livegrep.com](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep.com/tree/main/compose) in the `livegrep/livegrep.com` repository. ## Resource Usage [Permalink: Resource Usage](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep#resource-usage) livegrep builds an index file of your source code, and then works entirely out of that index, with no further access to the original git repositories. The index file will vary somewhat in size, but will usually be 3-5x the size of the indexed text. `livegrep` memory-maps the index file into RAM, so it can work out of index files larger than (available) RAM, but will perform better if the file can be loaded entirely into memory. Barring that, keeping the disk on fast SSDs is recommended for optimal performance. ## Regex Support [Permalink: Regex Support](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep#regex-support) Livegrep uses Google's [re2](https://github.com/google/re2) regular expression engine, and inherits its [supported\\ syntax](https://github.com/google/re2/wiki/Syntax). RE2 is mostly PCRE-compatible, but with some [mostly-deliberate\\ exceptions](https://swtch.com/~rsc/regexp/regexp3.html#caveats) ## LICENSE [Permalink: LICENSE](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep#license) Livegrep is open source. See [COPYING](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/blob/main/COPYING) for more information. ## About Interactively grep source code. Source for [http://livegrep.com/](http://livegrep.com/) ### Resources [Readme](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep#readme-ov-file) ### License [View license](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep#License-1-ov-file) ### Uh oh! There was an error while loading. [Please reload this page](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep). [Activity](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/activity) [Custom properties](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/custom-properties) ### Stars [**2.2k**\\ stars](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/stargazers) ### Watchers [**28**\\ watching](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/watchers) ### Forks [**200**\\ forks](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/forks) [Report repository](https://github.com/contact/report-content?content_url=https%3A%2F%2Fgithub.com%2Flivegrep%2Flivegrep&report=livegrep+%28user%29) ## [Releases](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/releases) No releases published ## [Packages\ 3](https://github.com/orgs/livegrep/packages?repo_name=livegrep) - [livegrep/base](https://github.com/orgs/livegrep/packages/container/package/livegrep%2Fbase) - [livegrep/indexer](https://github.com/orgs/livegrep/packages/container/package/livegrep%2Findexer) - [livegrep/nginx](https://github.com/orgs/livegrep/packages/container/package/livegrep%2Fnginx) ### Uh oh! View all files | ## Repository files navigation # Livegrep [![Build Status](https://camo.githubusercontent.com/d6de852cd5c32cb1895ded1271dfc48f632eefdacdcf41e8328b11fae06f471b/68747470733a2f2f636972636c6563692e636f6d2f67682f6c697665677265702f6c697665677265702e706e673f6272616e63683d6d6173746572)](https://circleci.com/gh/livegrep/livegrep) [Permalink: Livegrep ](https://github.com/ni/codesearch-livegrep#livegrep-) Livegrep is a tool, partially inspired by Google Code Search, for interactive regex search of ~gigabyte-scale source repositories. You can see a running instance at [http://livegrep.com/](http://livegrep.com/). ## Building [Permalink: Building](https://github.com/ni/codesearch-livegrep#building) livegrep builds using [bazel](http://www.bazel.io/). You will need to [install bazel](http://www.bazel.io/docs/install.html) version 0.3 or newer. livegrep vendors and/or fetches all of its dependencies using `bazel`, and so should only require a relatively recent C++ compiler to build. Once you have those dependencies, you can build using ``` bazel build //... ``` Note that the initial build will download around 100M of dependencies. These will be cached once downloaded. ## Invoking [Permalink: Invoking](https://github.com/ni/codesearch-livegrep#invoking) To run `livegrep`, you need to invoke both the `codesearch` backend index/search process, and the `livegrep` web interface. To run the sample web interface over livegrep itself, once you have built both `codesearch` and `livegrep`: In one terminal, start the `codesearch` server like so: ``` bazel-bin/src/tools/codesearch -grpc localhost:9999 doc/examples/livegrep/index.json ``` In another, run livegrep: ``` bazel-bin/cmd/livegrep/livegrep ``` In a browser, now visit [http://localhost:8910/](http://localhost:8910/), and you should see a working livegrep. ## Using Index Files [Permalink: Using Index Files](https://github.com/ni/codesearch-livegrep#using-index-files) The `codesearch` binary is responsible for reading source code, maintaining an index, and handling searches. `livegrep` is stateless and relies only on the connection to `codesearch` over a TCP connection. By default, `codesearch` will build an in-memory index over the repositories specified in its configuration file. You can, however, also instruct it to save the index to a file on disk. This the dual advantages of allowing indexes that are too large to fit in RAM, and of allowing an index file to be reused. You instruct `codesearch` to generate an index file via the `-dump_index` flag and to not launch a search server via the `-index_only` flag: ``` bazel-bin/src/tools/codesearch -index_only -dump_index livegrep.idx doc/examples/livegrep/index.json ``` Once `codeseach` has built the index, this index file can be used for future runs. Index files are standalone, and you no longer need access to the source code repositories, or even a configuration file, once an index has been built. You can just launch a search server like so: ``` bazel-bin/src/tools/codesearch -load_index livegrep.idx -grpc localhost:9999 ``` ## `livegrep` [Permalink: livegrep](https://github.com/ni/codesearch-livegrep#livegrep) The `livegrep` frontend expects an optional position argument indicating a JSON configuration file; See [doc/examples/livegrep/server.json](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/blob/master/doc/examples/livegrep/server.json) for an example, and [server/config/config.go](https://github.com/livegrep/livegrep/blob/master/server/config/config.go) for documentation of available options. By default, `livegrep` will connect to a single local codesearch instance on port `9999`, and listen for HTTP connections on port `8910`. ## github integration [Permalink: github integration](https://github.com/ni/codesearch-livegrep#github-integration) `livegrep` includes a helper driver, `livegrep-github-reindex`, which can automatically update and index selected github repositories. To download and index all of my repositories (except for forks), storing the repos in `repos/` and writing `nelhage.idx`, you might run: ``` bazel-bin/cmd/livegrep-github-reindex/livegrep-github-reindex -user=nelhage -forks=false -name=github.com/nelhage -out nelhage.idx ``` You can now use `nelhage.idx` as an argument to `codesearch -load_index`. ## Resource Usage [Permalink: Resource Usage](https://github.com/ni/codesearch-livegrep#resource-usage) livegrep builds an index file of your source code, and then works entirely out of that index, with no further access to the original git repositories. In general, the index file will be approximately the same size as the original source code. livegrep memory-maps the index file into RAM, so it should be able to work out of index files larger than (available) RAM, but will perform much better if the file can be loaded entirely into memory. ## LICENSE [Permalink: LICENSE](https://github.com/ni/codesearch-livegrep#license) Livegrep is open source. See COPYING for more information. ## About Interactively grep source code. Source for [http://livegrep.com/](http://livegrep.com/) ### Resources [Readme](https://github.com/ni/codesearch-livegrep#readme-ov-file) ### License [View license](https://github.com/ni/codesearch-livegrep#License-1-ov-file) ### Uh oh! web.title: code search
web.description: search · about · source · feedback; livegrep is a project by Nelson Elhage. Query: Special terms: path: -path: repo: -repo: max_matches: Case: match auto [?
web.markdown:
Query:

Special terms:
`path:``-path:``repo:``-repo:``max_matches:`

Case:matchauto
\[?\
\
Case-sensitive if the query contains capital letters\
\
\]
ignore

Regex:on

Search:Linux 6.19ML Libraries

Repo:

(all repositories)

Select AllDeselect All

- torvalds/
- linux

linux

Context:on

##### Special query terms

|     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `path:` | Only include results from matching files. | [example](https://livegrep.com/search?q=hello+path:test) |
| `-path:` | Exclude results from matching files. | [example](https://livegrep.com/search?q=hello+-path:test) |
| `repo:` | Only include results from matching repositories. | [example](https://livegrep.com/search?q=hello+repo:torvalds%2flinux) |
| `-repo:` | Exclude results from matching repositories. | [example](https://livegrep.com/search?q=hello+-repo:torvalds%2flinux) |
| `max_matches:` | Adjust the limit on number of matching lines returned. | [example](https://livegrep.com/search?q=hello+max_matches:5) |
| `(special-term:)` | Escape one of the above terms by wrapping it in parentheses (with regex enabled). | [example](https://livegrep.com/search?q=(file:)&regex=true) |

##### Regular Expressions

See [the RE2\\
documentation](https://github.com/google/re2/wiki/Syntax) for a complete listing of supported regex syntax.
